revision.text:
[[fr:Kakogawa matsuri]][[ja:播州の秋祭り]] [[File:播州の秋祭りの様子 (正八幡神社秋祭り).jpg|thumb|240px|正八幡神社秋祭り]] [[File:Nadanokenkamatsuri09.jpg|thumb|240px|灘のけんか祭り]] '''Banshū no Autumn Matsuri''' (播州の秋祭り) refers to various autumn festivals held at shrines in the Banshū region of southwestern Hyōgo Prefecture. These festivals are known for their large and small processions of floats (''taiko-dai''), which are paraded around the area. == Overview== Many of the festivals take the form of ''shinkō-sai'' (神幸祭), where a portable shrine (''mikoshi'') is carried to a temporary shrine (''otabisho''). The floats are often similar in shape to the mikoshi, though they do not function as sacred vessels but serve to accompany the mikoshi and entertain during the procession. These floats are commonly referred to as "yatai" or "taiko" and are also known by other names such as "Yassa," "Yattai," and "Taiko." Historically, the floats were sometimes referred to as "O-saki Daiko," "Ietai," "Yatai," or "Yassadaiko," and processions using floats are known to have taken place around the 18th century. However, the exact origin of these processions remains unclear. Similar structures called "dashi" are also referred to as ''taiko-dai.'' === Festival Timing=== Typically, these festivals are held annually in October, though they may also take place outside this month depending on local events or commemoration dates. Due to social circumstances, some festivals may be canceled or postponed, as seen in 1988 due to the illness of Emperor Showa and in 2020 and 2021 due to the COVID-19 pandemic. === Festival Regions=== The Banshū Autumn Festivals primarily take place in the Harima region but can also be seen in other areas such as Tajima (e.g., Asago City) and Kobe, particularly in the Tarumi and Nishi wards. These areas are historically linked to the Harima Province. In addition to Hyōgo Prefecture, similar festivals can be found in the Mimasaka region of Okayama Prefecture (e.g., Mimasaka City). The presence of floats in Mimasaka is likely influenced by its historical connection to the former Harima Province, particularly the town of Ōhara, which was a stop on the Sankin-kōtai route between Himeji and Tottori. === Attire and Other Details=== Festival participants typically wear a uniform attire, such as ''hifu'' (法被) or ''juban'' (襦袢), along with ''hachimaki'' (鉢巻) headbands and ''tabi'' socks. In some cases, float bearers (called ''neriko'') wear a traditional sumo-like garment known as the ''mawashi.'' The tradition of wearing the ''mawashi'' is unique to specific towns, like Himeji, Takasago, and Kakogawa. It is not commonly worn outside this region. Some festivals feature children dressed in lavish costumes, with some even mimicking the ''hitotsumono'' (一ツ物) tradition, where the children must avoid touching the ground. These festivals often attract amateur photographers, and the images are frequently published in camera magazines and railway publications. In addition to the floats, some regions feature ''dan-jiri'' floats, which are used for lion dances and artistic performances. Many of these dances are from the Ise Taigakara tradition, and the lion dancers are often accompanied by young boys dressed in lavish costumes. === Types of Floats=== File:Sumiyoshi-uozumi-maturi02.JPG|1. [[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Akashi)|Uozumi Sumiyoshi Shrine]]
Nishijima Yatai File:Sone nishinochou hutonyatai.JPG|2. Sone Tenmangu
Nishinocho Yatai File:Nada no Kenka matsuri 13.jpg|3. Nada's Kenka Matsuri
Matsubara Yatai File:甲八幡神社 秋祭り 本宮 練り合わせの様子.jpg|4. Kō Hachiman Shrine
Sakai Village, Hashizume Village, Iwaya Village Yatai Procession File:Hamanomiya daibasashi.JPG|5. Hamanomiya Tenmangu
Tenjin Yatai File:Usukihachiman itoi 01.jpg|6. Usuki Hachiman Shrine
Itoi Yatai The floats in Banshū vary in style but are generally categorized into two types based on their roof designs: futon-roofed and mikoshi-roofed floats. Futon-style floats are common in eastern and northern Harima, while mikoshi-roofed floats are more common in central and western Harima. === Festival Activities=== Different areas feature different styles of float processions, including the ''neri'' (float procession), where several floats are combined for a larger procession, and ''daiba-nori,'' where only the base of the float is carried. Other activities include ''chōsā'' (throwing the float), where the floats are raised and lowered dramatically. The standard cheer for floats is "Yō Iya Sā," not "Wasshoi," which is common in other festivals. Some regions also use other chants like "Chōsā." === Shrines and Rituals=== {| class="wikitable" |+ |- style="background-color: =ccc;" ! Shrine !! Festival Date !! Location !! Remarks |- |[[Amachi Hachiman Shrine]]||1st or 2nd Sunday of September||{{ill|Kanzaki District|en|Kanzaki District|qid=Q1207192|lt=Kanzaki District|1=Kanzaki District}} [[Ichikawacho]]
Amachi||"Lion Dance," Prefecture-Designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Asset |- |[[Ikawadani Sōsha]]||First Saturday and Sunday in October||Kobe City, Nishi Ward Ikawadani-cho
Uewaki 1005||Saturday: evening festival, Sunday: daytime festival. 4 floats gather. |- |[[Wakamiyasan Shrine]]||1st Sunday of October||[[Miki City]] [[Yoshikawa Town]]
Inada 557||"Yahō Ceremony," Prefecture-Designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Asset |- |[[Takaoka Shrine (Inari Daimyojin)]]||1st Sunday of October||[[Kato City]] Takaoka||"Taiko 3 floats" |- |[[Kamogawa Sumiyoshi Shrine]]||October 4-5 (2011: Oct 1-2)||[[Kato City]] Kamogawa
571||"Shinji Dance," National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Asset |- |[[Emishukuni Tenmangu Shrine]]||October 8-9||[[Himeji City]] [[Shikama Ward]]
Emishu 14||24 participants in ''neriko'' carry the float "Daiba Nori." Prefecture-Designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Asset |- |[[Hamanomiya Tenmangu Shrine]]||October 8-9||Himeji City Shikama Ward
Suka 40||24 participants in ''neriko'' carry the float "Daiba Sashi," Prefecture-Designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Asset. "Kansen no Kami" (Shinto inscription) from 1797 |- |[[Kamada Shrine]]||Weekend before Sports Day||Himeji City Hirakata Ward
Kamada||Flags and procession with 4 children floats and 9 large floats. }} [[ja:播州の秋祭り]] [[fr:Kakogawa matsuri]] |} == References == {{translated page|ja|播州の秋祭り|version=103162820|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q11498705}} [[Category:兵庫県の祭り]] [[Category:Float festivals of Japan]] [[Category:Harima Province]]
